stories.headline: Trump names housing-finance chief Bill Pulte acting Director of National Intelligence, replacing Tulsi Gabbard
stories.summary: President Trump appointed Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte as acting Director of National Intelligence, replacing Tulsi Gabbard, who is departing June 30 citing her husband's cancer diagnosis. Pulte, a Trump ally with no intelligence background, will keep his FHFA and Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac roles. Democrats blasted the pick; Republicans defended it.

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames an unqualified loyalist handed the intelligence community, fueling fears of politicized spy agencies.
perspectives.summary: The Hill reports Democrats erupted over the choice, with Sen. Chuck Schumer calling Pulte a 'partisan thug with no experience in intelligence' and other members warning that dual-hatting the housing-finance chief atop the ODNI risks bending the CIA and NSA to the president's political ends.

sources.title: 'Partisan thug': Democrats erupt over Trump intel pick Bill Pulte

perspectives.keyFraming: Straight reporting on the appointment, Pulte's lack of intelligence experience and his dual role at FHFA.
perspectives.summary: NPR and CNBC report Trump named Pulte acting DNI to succeed Tulsi Gabbard, who is leaving at the end of June, noting Pulte has no intelligence background and will continue running the Federal Housing Finance Agency and overseeing Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac alongside the new role.

sources.title: Trump appoints Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence
Trump names housing chief Bill Pulte acting intelligence director, replacing Tulsi Gabbard

perspectives.keyFraming: Casts the move as a decisive Trump loyalist appointment and mocks the Democratic reaction.
perspectives.summary: Fox News frames Pulte's elevation as a confident pick of a proven Trump ally to steady the intelligence community after Gabbard's exit, highlighting his record running housing finance and treating Democratic objections as predictable political theater.

sources.title: Pulte tapped as acting intelligence director to replace Tulsi Gabbard

stories.headline: Israel strikes Tyre district in Lebanon's deadliest day since the ceasefire as it expands its occupation
stories.summary: Israeli strikes across south Lebanon on June 2 killed more than a dozen people in what local officials called the deadliest day since the November ceasefire. The IDF said it hit over 100 targets, killed 20-plus militants north of the Litani River, and ordered evacuations in four areas of Tyre district, warning of Hezbollah fighters it says are embedded there. A hospital in Tyre was damaged; the IDF said it was 'not targeted.'

perspectives.keyFraming: IDF frames the strikes as removing a direct Hezbollah threat to Galilee and blames militant embedding in civilian areas.
perspectives.summary: The Jerusalem Post relays the IDF account that its forces struck more than 100 targets and killed over 20 militants north of the Litani, ordered evacuations in Tyre district, and said a damaged Tyre hospital was 'not targeted,' arguing Hezbollah operatives embedded among civilians bear responsibility for the toll.

sources.title: IDF says hospital damaged after strikes in Tyre, Lebanon, 'was not targeted'

perspectives.keyFraming: Explains the escalation a day after Trump announced an Israel-Hezbollah understanding to stop fighting.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera lays out what is known after Trump said Israel and Hezbollah would halt hostilities, noting the renewed strikes on Tyre and the south came almost immediately, with casualty reports and evacuation orders complicating claims that a durable de-escalation was taking hold.

sources.title: Trump says Israel, Hezbollah to stop fighting: What we know

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames the killings and occupation expansion as ceasefire violations driving regional escalation.
perspectives.summary: Democracy Now centers the civilian toll, reporting Israeli forces killed more people in southern Lebanon and expanded their occupation even as Iran suspended talks with Washington, presenting the day's strikes as part of a pattern of escalation rather than enforcement of a truce.

sources.title: Iran Suspends U.S. Talks as Israel Kills More in Lebanon & Expands Occupation

stories.headline: Alphabet to raise about $80 billion in equity for AI build-out, with Berkshire Hathaway anchoring $10 billion
stories.summary: Alphabet said on June 1 it plans to raise roughly $80 billion through equity offerings to fund AI compute infrastructure — its first straight equity raise since going public in 2004. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway anchored the deal with a $10 billion private placement, split between Class A and Class C shares. The move by a cash-rich mega-cap underscored the scale of capital the AI build-out now demands.

perspectives.keyFraming: Mechanics: a cash-rich Google tapping equity markets signals how capital-intensive the AI race has become.
perspectives.summary: Bloomberg and CNBC report Alphabet will raise about $80 billion in equity to fund AI infrastructure, with Berkshire Hathaway taking a $10 billion private placement across Class A and Class C shares — a notable raise for a company that has generated ample cash and rarely issued stock since its 2004 IPO.

sources.title: Alphabet's $80 Billion AI Raise Gets $10 Billion Berkshire Bet
Berkshire Hathaway invests extra $10 billion in Alphabet, deepening bet on AI

perspectives.keyFraming: Retail investors parse Berkshire deepening its biggest AI bet and the optics of a rare Alphabet equity raise.
perspectives.summary: Yahoo Finance highlights retail and investor reaction to Berkshire's added $10 billion stake in Alphabet, framing it as a major AI bet by Warren Buffett's company and debating what an $80 billion equity raise says about the cost of keeping pace in AI compute.

sources.title: Berkshire to invest $10 billion in Alphabet in major AI bet

stories.headline: Rubio tells Senate that US-Iran nuclear and Hormuz talks are at an impasse as Tehran halts messaging
stories.summary: Testifying to the Senate on June 2 — his first appearance since the Iran war began — Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the US is still in contact with Iran and there is 'the prospect' Tehran could negotiate aspects of its nuclear program, but set conditions: Iran must declare the Strait of Hormuz open, end tolls, help clear mines and not fire on commercial ships. Iran's Fars agency said the sides stopped exchanging messages days earlier; PBS called the talks an impasse.

perspectives.keyFraming: Reports Rubio holding open the prospect of nuclear talks while attaching hard conditions on the Strait of Hormuz.
perspectives.summary: CNBC and PBS NewsHour report Rubio told senators the administration remains in contact with Iran and sees a prospect of negotiating its nuclear program, but only if Tehran declares Hormuz open, stops charging tolls, helps clear mines and pledges not to fire on commercial vessels — with both outlets characterizing the current state of talks as an impasse.

sources.title: Rubio says U.S. is in talks with Iran over nuclear program as senators press for war endgame
U.S.-Iran talks at impasse over nuclear program and Strait of Hormuz

perspectives.keyFraming: Centers Iran's account that it suspended messaging because of Israel's Lebanon assault, not nuclear brinkmanship.
perspectives.summary: Democracy Now frames the breakdown from Tehran's vantage point, reporting Iran suspended its exchange of messages with Washington over Israel's expanding operations in Lebanon and Gaza, and tying the standoff to the wider regional escalation rather than to US demands over the Strait of Hormuz alone.

sources.title: Iran Suspends U.S. Talks as Israel Kills More in Lebanon & Expands Occupation

stories.headline: Acting AG Blanche tells Congress DOJ won't pursue $1.8B 'anti-weaponization' fund; Trump tax-audit ban stays
stories.summary: At a June 2 House Appropriations subcommittee hearing, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said the Justice Department is 'not moving forward' with the proposed $1.78 billion 'anti-weaponization' settlement fund, answering 'Correct' when asked if that meant never. Speaker Mike Johnson called the idea 'off the table.' Blanche also said an arrangement barring IRS audits of Trump and his businesses remains in place.

perspectives.keyFraming: Emphasizes the fund's death alongside the still-standing IRS audit shield protecting Trump.
perspectives.summary: MSNBC's hearing coverage frames Blanche as driving the final nail into the contested settlement fund while pressing on the lingering agreement that shields Trump and his companies from IRS audits, casting both as favors to the president that Congress should scrutinize.

sources.title: Blanche hearing takeaways: Acting AG drives nail into 'anti-weaponization' fund

perspectives.keyFraming: Neutral hearing takeaways: the fund is over and the ban on Trump tax audits remains.
perspectives.summary: CNN and Roll Call report Blanche told the appropriations subcommittee the department will not move forward with the $1.78 billion fund and, asked whether that was permanent, replied 'Correct,' while confirming that an arrangement barring IRS audits of Trump and his businesses is still in effect.

sources.title: Takeaways from Blanche's House testimony: 'Anti-weaponization' fund is over, ban on Trump tax audits remains
Blanche says 'anti-weaponization' fund not moving forward

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames the fund as dead for good and centers the GOP-DOJ resolution of the dispute.
perspectives.summary: The Washington Examiner reports Blanche told lawmakers the anti-weaponization fund is off the table for good after Republican backlash, with Speaker Mike Johnson saying it would be a 'difficult prospect' given vote tallies, presenting the outcome as the administration backing down on a politically costly idea.

sources.title: Blanche: DOJ will not move forward with anti-weaponization fund
